text: What application generates and manages your work orders today?

likely_answers.answer_text: SAP PM (within our ERP)
likely_answers.implication: Data migration complexity high; mobile gap likely; scheduling optimization gap almost certain
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about SAP version and whether they have a separate scheduling tool

likely_answers.answer_text: IBM Maximo
likely_answers.implication: EAM-centric — likely missing mobile-first dispatch optimization; assess Maximo version (on-prem vs. MAS)
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about Maximo version and mobile field worker app currently in use

likely_answers.answer_text: Oracle Utilities Work Management
likely_answers.implication: Utility-native WM — assess integration with OMS and GIS; check if upgrade to cloud version is planned
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about OMS integration and whether crew scheduling is separate

likely_answers.answer_text: We use spreadsheets / manual dispatch
likely_answers.implication: Greenfield opportunity — high urgency; operational risk is high; strong ROI case for modern FSM
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Understand scale: how many work orders per day, crew size

likely_answers.answer_text: Salesforce Field Service or ClickSoftware
likely_answers.implication: Modern FSM already in place — focus on optimization, GIS integration depth, and utilization
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about pain points with current implementation

text: How do you currently schedule and dispatch field crews to work orders?

likely_answers.answer_text: Dispatchers manually assign work orders in the morning
likely_answers.implication: Major scheduling optimization opportunity; likely high idle time and inefficient routing
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about dispatcher-to-crew ratio and average drive time

likely_answers.answer_text: We have an automated scheduling engine (ClickSoftware, etc.)
likely_answers.implication: Scheduling baseline exists — focus on optimization quality, real-time re-routing, and GIS integration depth
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about override rate (how often dispatchers manually override the algorithm)

likely_answers.answer_text: Field supervisors self-assign from a shared queue
likely_answers.implication: Decentralized dispatch — inconsistent prioritization, likely SLA risk on priority work
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about SAIDI impact of delayed priority work

text: What mobile application do your field technicians use today to receive and complete work orders?

likely_answers.answer_text: They call dispatch or use radio
likely_answers.implication: No mobile FSM app — significant productivity gap; strong ROI opportunity for mobile deployment
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about proportion of crews with smartphones and connectivity coverage in service territory

likely_answers.answer_text: Custom-built in-house mobile app
likely_answers.implication: High technical debt risk; ask about iOS/Android split, offline capability, and maintenance burden
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Understand who maintains the app and what the development backlog looks like

likely_answers.answer_text: Salesforce Field Service mobile / ServiceMax
likely_answers.implication: Modern mobile baseline — assess offline capability gaps, GIS data available offline, safety form completeness
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about offline mode usage and most common pain points

likely_answers.answer_text: Rugged device with legacy Windows app
likely_answers.implication: Aging mobile platform — likely EOL risk; user adoption will be key challenge in any replacement
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about device refresh cycle and user sentiment toward mobile tools

text: How is your FSM integrated with your GIS system today?

likely_answers.answer_text: No GIS integration — crews look up asset locations separately
likely_answers.implication: Major GIS integration gap — crews lack asset context in the field; routing inefficiency; asset record accuracy risk after field work
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask which GIS platform they use and whether they have ArcGIS or Smallworld

likely_answers.answer_text: Partial integration — GIS shows work location but no asset hierarchy
likely_answers.implication: Partial integration — assess gap between work location visibility and full asset context (drawings, history, network model)
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask whether crews can see upstream network topology for outage isolation

likely_answers.answer_text: Tight bidirectional integration with ArcGIS
likely_answers.implication: GIS integration is mature — focus shifts to advanced use cases: offline GIS data on mobile, outage polygon display, predictive routing
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about GIS write-back quality after field work completion

text: Do your field technicians work in areas where cellular connectivity is unreliable or unavailable?

likely_answers.answer_text: Yes — significant portions of our territory have poor or no coverage
likely_answers.implication: Offline mobile capability is a must-have, not a nice-to-have. Assess which FSM platforms support full offline WO lifecycle.
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about specific geography (rural, underground, substations) and what data crews need offline

likely_answers.answer_text: Mostly covered — occasional dead zones
likely_answers.implication: Offline capability important but not blocking. Assess what data is needed in dead zones (asset drawings, safety plans).
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about crew behavior when they lose connectivity mid-work-order

likely_answers.answer_text: Full coverage — FirstNet/LTE across our territory
likely_answers.implication: Connectivity not a constraint — focus on app performance and data synchronization speed rather than offline architecture
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Verify coverage extends to underground and substation environments

text: How do you manage third-party contractor crews in your field service workflow today?

likely_answers.answer_text: Contractors are managed outside our FSM — separate spreadsheet or portal
likely_answers.implication: Contractor visibility gap — no real-time status on contractor work; billing reconciliation is manual; compliance risk
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask what percentage of field work volume is contractor-executed and the primary work types

likely_answers.answer_text: Contractors use the same FSM system as our employees
likely_answers.implication: Contractor integration is mature — assess data segregation, credential management, and performance reporting
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask how contractor user licenses are managed and whether NERC CIP access is restricted

likely_answers.answer_text: We manage through contractor-owned apps and send paper work orders
likely_answers.implication: No digital integration with contractors — significant efficiency and compliance gap; storm surge management risk
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about contractor volume during storm events and reconciliation process

text: How do you track and report your SAIDI and SAIFI reliability performance, and where does the data originate?

likely_answers.answer_text: We pull outage data from our OMS and calculate SAIDI/SAIFI in a data warehouse or spreadsheet
likely_answers.implication: Manual SAIDI reporting process — assess data latency, accuracy risk, and regulatory reporting burden
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about Major Event Day identification process and how FSM data is reconciled with OMS outage records

likely_answers.answer_text: Our OMS calculates SAIDI/SAIFI automatically and feeds our regulatory reporting tool
likely_answers.implication: Automated reliability reporting — assess whether FSM event data (restoration timestamps) feeds cleanly into OMS
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about the FSM-to-OMS data latency for restoration confirmations

likely_answers.answer_text: We are not sure — different departments own different parts of the data
likely_answers.implication: Data governance gap for regulatory reporting — significant compliance risk; opportunity for FSM-OMS integration to create single source of truth
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Understand who is accountable to the state PUC for reliability reporting

text: Are your field crews subject to NERC CIP access control requirements, and how is that enforced in your field workflows?

likely_answers.answer_text: Yes — we have critical cyber assets and physical access requirements under NERC CIP-006
likely_answers.implication: NERC CIP compliance adds significant complexity to FSM work order authorization and contractor management. FSM must support CIP access logging.
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask how work authorization to CIP-protected substations is currently tracked and whether the FSM captures access logs

likely_answers.answer_text: We are a distribution-only utility — NERC CIP does not apply to us
likely_answers.implication: NERC CIP not a constraint — simplifies contractor management and field access workflow design
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Verify whether any shared infrastructure with a CIP-covered transmission entity creates indirect requirements

text: Do you have AMI (smart meters) deployed, and are outage notifications from smart meters integrated into your work order process?

likely_answers.answer_text: Yes — AMI deployed and outage pings automatically create trouble orders in our OMS
likely_answers.implication: AMI-to-OMS-to-FSM pipeline exists — assess latency, false positive rate, and whether FSM dispatches based on AMI-generated trouble orders without dispatcher review
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about false positive rate and whether dispatchers review AMI-generated trouble orders before dispatching crew

likely_answers.answer_text: AMI deployed but not connected to our OMS or work order system
likely_answers.implication: Significant integration gap — AMI data available but not actionable. Major opportunity to reduce SAIDI through faster outage detection-to-dispatch
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about the roadmap for AMI-OMS integration and who owns the project

likely_answers.answer_text: No AMI — we still use analog meters
likely_answers.implication: Outage detection dependent on customer calls — SAIDI impact significant; AMI roadmap is likely on the agenda
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about AMI plans and CPUC/state PUC requirements for smart meter deployment

text: How are outage response work orders created and routed to field crews during a storm event?

likely_answers.answer_text: OMS automatically creates trouble orders and dispatches to available crews via our FSM
likely_answers.implication: Integrated outage response pipeline — assess how mutual aid crew management works and whether contractor crews are included in the dispatch pool during storms
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about storm crew surge capacity and how mutual aid agreements are operationalized in the FSM

likely_answers.answer_text: Dispatchers manually create trouble orders based on OMS alarm dashboard
likely_answers.implication: Manual outage response workflow — significant SAIDI risk during high-volume storm events; dispatcher overload is a known failure mode
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about the worst storm experience: how many trouble orders were created in a single shift and how many dispatchers were working

likely_answers.answer_text: We don't have an OMS — outage response is driven by customer calls
likely_answers.implication: No OMS — highest SAIDI risk profile; outage detection latency directly inflates SAIDI; OMS and FSM are joint priorities
likely_answers.follow_up_note: Ask about average time from customer call to crew dispatch and how customer outage maps are maintained
